rationale_for_baseline_tier: An infected diabetic foot ulcer (erythema, induration, purulent exudate) on an insensate foot, with symptomatic hyperglycaemia and mild systemic upset, is a limb-threatening problem that requires urgent same-day in-person assessment — hands-on wound evaluation, empirical antibiotics, offloading and urgent bloods that telehealth cannot provide. The source note itself mandated immediate out-of-hours assessment. The patient is currently haemodynamically stable with localised infection and no ascending lymphangitis, no rigors, and no features of a hyperglycaemic emergency, so T4/T5 is not warranted at baseline — the T3 ceiling for a telehealth uplift is correct. Escalation to ED (T4) or 000 (T5) is reserved for the specific spreading-infection/sepsis or hyperglycaemic-emergency triggers below. Under-triaging this to routine or next-week GP care (T1/T2) would risk progression to osteomyelitis, sepsis and amputation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading up the leg, the whole foot becoming hot and swollen, or worsening pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the emergency department the same day / immediately if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Ascending cellulitis or a rapidly worsening deep infection in a diabetic foot can become limb- and life-threatening quickly and needs emergency in-person care and IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading up your leg, or your whole foot becomes hot, swollen and more painful, go straight to the emergency department — don't wait for the GP review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with shaking chills (rigors), feeling very unwell, or a racing heart — features of systemic infection/sepsis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately; call 000 instead if he becomes drowsy, confused, or is collapsing
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features signal spreading infection or sepsis; diabetics can deteriorate rapidly with a blunted response, so the threshold to attend ED is low, and 000 is warranted if consciousness is affected.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a high fever with shaking chills, feel very unwell or your heart is racing, get to an emergency department urgently. If you become drowsy or confused, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Drowsiness or confusion, persistent vomiting, rapid deep breathing, fruity-smelling breath, or severe worsening thirst — possible hyperglycaemic emergency (DKA/HHS)
escalation_edges.rationale: In newly uncontrolled diabetes an intercurrent infection can precipitate DKA or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state — a medical emergency requiring ambulance transport and IV therapy the patient cannot self-administer.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become drowsy or confused, start vomiting and can't keep fluids down, are breathing fast and deep, or your breath smells fruity, call 000 straight away — this can be a diabetic emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to obtain a same-day in-person assessment for the infected foot
escalation_edges.rationale: The infected foot must be seen in person today; if the usual GP cannot fit him in, an out-of-hours service, urgent care centre or ED is the fallback rather than waiting.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You need this foot seen in person today. If your GP can't see you, go to an urgent care centre or the emergency department — please don't leave it until tomorrow.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Peripheral neuropathy — the foot is insensate, so pain will not reliably warn of worsening infection
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate and rely on visual signs (spreading redness, swelling, discharge, fever) rather than pain; emphasise daily visual foot checks.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and was by himself at the time of the call
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure he has a clear plan and a way to get to in-person care today; advise having someone check on him and confirm he can recognise and act on the escalation triggers alone.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Limping and functionally limited by fatigue and foot pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Consider transport assistance to the same-day assessment; offloading with crutches must not compromise his ability to get to care.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs to be seen in person today. Arrange an urgent same-day appointment with a GP, out-of-hours service or urgent care centre for the infected foot — they will assess the wound, start antibiotics, arrange offloading and organise urgent blood tests. Keep all weight off the foot using the crutches, keep the dressing clean and dry, and do not walk barefoot. Attend fasting for the arranged blood tests.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day in-person GP / out-of-hours service / urgent care centre
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is out of clinic hours, use an out-of-hours GP service or an urgent care centre; go to the emergency department if the infection is spreading or you feel systemically unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the assessing clinician with the wound history (2-week non-healing plantar wound now purulent), the systemic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, stocking-distribution numbness), and the request for urgent fasting glucose/HbA1c and renal function so glucose-lowering therapy can be started safely.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an infected diabetic foot ulcer as a minor wound suitable for self-care or routine GP review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delay allows progression to osteomyelitis, spreading cellulitis or sepsis and potential lower-limb amputation.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising a next-day/48-hour GP appointment without securing same-day in-person assessment of the infected foot; an untreated infected neuropathic foot can deteriorate within a day.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a stable, localised foot infection with no systemic or hyperglycaemic-emergency features — unnecessary emergency resource use with low patient harm.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot grade the true depth/extent of the foot infection, cannot confirm diabetes, and cannot measure vitals or glucose — the severity could be worse than it appears on video.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to urgent same-day in-person assessment, give explicit worsening triggers for ED/000, and avoid any remote diagnostic confirmation or prescribing.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassuring the patient or deferring to routine review because the visible signs look localised and the reported fever is mild.
